coredata.dc:title: Physical and mechanical properties of fused deposition modelling PLA/carbon dot nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Campuzano, Alberto Jorge Baeza
Rezende, Rafael Barbosa
Taborda, Nestor Cifuentes
dos Santos, Júlio Cesar
Pereira, Fabiano Vargas
Panzera, Tulio Hallak
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the incorporation of carbon dots (CDs) into polylactic acid (PLA) filaments to improve their mechanical properties for fused deposition modelling (FDM). Known for their adjustable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, low toxicity, and biocompatibility, CDs are integrated into PLA at various concentrations (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 wt%). Samples reinforced with 0.1–0.7 wt% CDs exhibit a tensile modulus ranging from 3.55 to 4.0 GPa and a tensile strength from 30 to 35 MPa. Meanwhile, those with 3.0 wt% achieve a higher tensile modulus (4.3 GPa) and strength (55 MPa), albeit with noted manufacturing challenges. The inclusion of 5.0 wt% CDs compromises the filament manufacturing process, resulting in a reduced tensile stress of 27 MPa, lower than pristine PLA, and exhibiting micro defects after 3D printing. Fluorescence analysis during tensile testing indicates that lower CD concentrations reduce fluorescence, while higher concentrations increase it, suggesting a correlation with the mechanical response. PLA reinforced with 0.5 wt% CDs not only offers improved mechanical properties but also facilitates easy manufacturing, showing promise for creating both solid and lattice 3D-printed products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-screw extrusion
3d printing
Fluorescence analysis
Carbon dots
Failure analysis
Mechanical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Extrusion Process performed (a-c), homogenisation of PLA/CDs along the screw and die swell (d-f), full extrusion system (g, h) [24–26].
TGA/dTG analysis of carbon dots.
Micro-defects on the filament material and printed samples.
3D printed sample and dimensions.
Experimental set-up for the florescence and width evolution.
Ultra microhardness testing of filaments and 3D printed parts.
Digital microscope with polarised lenses.

Characterisation of the obtained CDs. (a) FTIR; (b) Raman spectrum; (c) UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths. The inset exhibits the sample under visible and UV light sources. (d) TEM microscopy image.
a) Fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the CDs, b) particle size distribution.
a) Mean tensile elastic modulus and b) strength of each condition.
Typical tensile stress-strain curves.
Fluorescence and sample width analysis for 0.1 and 0.3 wt% of carbon dots.
Fluorescence for 0.7 and 1.0 wt% of carbon dots.
Fluorescence signal level and typical failure modes.
Failure mode analysis through polarised lens.
Failure mode mechanism for 0.5 wt% condition.
Failure mechanism at the interfacial zone of 3D printed threads.
Young modulus, indentation depth and hardness Vickers obtained via dynamic ultra microhardness test (DUH).
Typical microvoids on sample surfaces.
Mean tensile mechanical properties and Tukey test.

head.ce:title: Physical and mechanical properties of fused deposition modelling PLA/carbon dot nanocomposites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry – Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais - UFMG

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science and Engineering – University of São Paulo – USP, Av. João Dagnone, 110, São Carlos, São Paulo 13563-120, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering – University of São Paulo – USP

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Material Science and Engineering – University of São Paulo – USP, Av. João Dagnone, 110, São Carlos, 13563-120, São Paulo, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the incorporation of carbon dots (CDs) into polylactic acid (PLA) filaments to improve their mechanical properties for fused deposition modelling (FDM). Known for their adjustable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, low toxicity, and biocompatibility, CDs are integrated into PLA at various concentrations (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 wt%). Samples reinforced with 0.1–0.7 wt% CDs exhibit a tensile modulus ranging from 3.55 to 4.0 GPa and a tensile strength from 30 to 35 MPa. Meanwhile, those with 3.0 wt% achieve a higher tensile modulus (4.3 GPa) and strength (55 MPa), albeit with noted manufacturing challenges. The inclusion of 5.0 wt% CDs compromises the filament manufacturing process, resulting in a reduced tensile stress of 27 MPa, lower than pristine PLA, and exhibiting micro defects after 3D printing. Fluorescence analysis during tensile testing indicates that lower CD concentrations reduce fluorescence, while higher concentrations increase it, suggesting a correlation with the mechanical response. PLA reinforced with 0.5 wt% CDs not only offers improved mechanical properties but also facilitates easy manufacturing, showing promise for creating both solid and lattice 3D-printed products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent exploration of carbon dots (CDs) in nanocomposites, particularly in conjunction with polycaprolactone (PCL) for tissue regeneration, has garnered significant attention. CDs are recognised for their inertness and stable fluorescence, rendering them promising for medical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, and scaffold stent utilisation [1,2]. Furthermore, the optoelectronic properties of the carbon dot composite enable their use in biosensors and bioelectronics [3]. In the realm of security, researchers are employing carbon dots for anti-counterfeit systems to detect forgery in banknotes [4], while ongoing work is focused on improving drug detection [5]. The integration of carbon nanoparticles with polymer materials has shown potential in enhancing the properties of nanocomposites, including biocompatibility, unique optical properties, and cost-effectiveness [6,7]. CDs have been utilised with PLA to create electrospun membranes for the development of innovative and sustainable oil absorbents [8], and in other applications, CDs combined with PLA have proven useful for fabricating face masks [9]. CDs demonstrate structural stability when employed within foam-based applications, as evidenced by recent research findings [10]. While these carbon nanoparticles may have some defects compared to graphene, they offer the advantage of being more affordable. Additionally, CDs present a wide variety of polar groups on their surface, making them easily dispersible in aqueous media and easier to synthesize compared to carbon nanotubes. It is important to note that all three materials, including carbon dots, graphene, and carbon nanotubes, have demonstrated improvements in the mechanical properties of polymers and other materials, making them promising candidates for various applications.

In the field of medical and bio applications, the combination of carbon nanoparticles with polymers has garnered significant attention. Nevertheless, achieving desired properties can be challenging with certain polymers due to their inherently simplistic molecular structures. As a result, there has been a shift towards utilising PLA as a viable alternative. PLA has been successfully used to fabricate stents through fused deposition filament, offering the advantages of biodegradability and improved mechanical response [11]. Despite these benefits, the fabrication of PLA-based filaments for stents remains a current challenge that researchers are actively working to overcome [11]. The extrusion process has been widely utilised for incorporating particles into polymers, to enhance mechanical properties for various applications.

Extrusion technologies, including single-screw, twin-screw, and fused deposition modelling, have been commonly employed due to their simplicity and effectiveness. For instance, the incorporation of carbon nanofibres at a concentration of 3 wt% into PLA has been found to substantially increase its resistance to tensile forces due to the alignment of the fibres [12]. Additionally, the single-screw extrusion process has been successfully employed for the reuse of copper waste material considering spherical-like particles in conjunction with PLA, resulting in improved mechanical properties, even after multiple extrusion, while presenting severe abrasion into the extruder components [13]. However, the incorporation of Carbon Dots and PLA into extrusion technology has yet to be explored, mainly because of the limited knowledge about how they impact the physical and mechanical properties of the matrix polymer. Twin-screw extrusion has also shown promise in incorporating polymer-cellulose nanofibres at varying percentages (1–5 wt%), leading to remarkable improvements in mechanical performance [14]. In addition to these common extrusion processes, pneumatic extrusion has been used for the homogenisation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes with PLA, resulting in a composite material with enhanced mechanical properties [15]. It is worth noting that direct extrusion is particularly useful for thermosensitive polymers. However, fast degradation can occur, especially in the case of PLA, highlighting the need to incorporate different fibres to mitigate this phenomenon, particularly during the fused deposition modelling process [16]. These findings highlight the potential of extrusion techniques for enhancing the mechanical properties of PLA-based composites.

Once the extrusion process is completed, FDM (Fused Deposition Modelling) technology allows for further homogenisation by introducing additional materials such as bronze powder, which has been shown to enhance the mechanical performance of the final product [17]. However, it is important to note that the FDM process can potentially degrade the machine components, necessitating further analysis. By using surface-treated fibres, significant improvements in mechanical properties have been observed. For example, the inclusion of short carbon fibres enhances tensile strength, although it may lead to a reduction in compressive strength. Nevertheless, when a coupling agent coating method is employed, the compressive strength surpasses that of pure PLA, and no degradation is observed during the homogenisation process in FDM [18]. In addition, specimens fabricated through FDM with the addition of different agents and varying reinforcements weight percentages have demonstrated improvements in the mechanical responses while lowering degradation [19–21].

In the current research landscape, there is a notable lack of understanding regarding the impact of CDs on PLA when utilising extrusion techniques, especially with single-screw machines. This marks the initial endeavour to employ extrusion technology in creating the PLA/Carbon dots nanocomposite. This work centres around the synthesis and characterisation of carbon dots, as well as their application as a reinforcing phase in extruded PLA filaments and 3D printed parts. The study investigates the impact of carbon dots on both the tensile mechanical properties and fluorescence signal levels of the resulting composites. Additionally, the failure phenomena will be analysed through microstructural analysis, providing a comprehensive overview of this exploration. By shedding light on these aspects, this research seeks to contribute to a deeper understanding of the interaction between carbon dots, PLA, and the extrusion process, ultimately paving the way for potential advancements in this field.

Citric acid (anhydrous) is obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, USA), while ethylenediamine is acquired from Neon (Suzano, Brazil). The PLA pellets, with a size range of 3–5 mm, are supplied by the company 3Dlab®. All materials are used without further purification.

The carbon dots (CDs) are prepared according to the scalable method described by Wang et al. [22] with minor modifications. Firstly, 25.12 g of citric acid and 8.74 g of ethylenediamine are added to a 125 mL round-bottom flask. The mixture is then heated to 150°C under constant magnetic stirring. Subsequently, the system is refluxed for 90 min until the resultant product exhibits a brown hue. Finally, the product is cooled to room temperature to complete the preparation process.

The filament fabrication process is performed using the desktop extruder PRO 350/200 EX supplied by FilaFab®. This extruder is a single screw with an auger speed ranging from 5 to 30 RPM, a torque of 45 N.m and an extrusion rate of 800 g/hour. The carbon dots are combined with PLA at seven different concentrations: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 3.0 and 5.0 wt%. The process begins by placing PLA and CDs in a clean container and hand mixing them for 10 min. The carbon dots adhere to the surface of the pellets through electrostatic forces, as illustrated in Fig. 1a. The mixture is poured into the extruder feeder and processed at a constant speed of 15 RPM and a temperature of 130 °C. This temperature level, as indicated by the TGA analysis ( Fig. 2), does not degrade the PLA [23] or the carbon dots. During this stage, the PLA molecules create space for the carbon dots, promoting the homogenisation of the components, as depicted in Fig. 1b. A full homogenisation is achieved in the last extruder section through the elevated temperature and shearing mixing process [24]. The spaces between the chains are significantly reduced, as shown in Fig. 1c. As the material is pushed out by the screw [25], it enters a contraction provided by the heated nozzle, causing a re-circulation phenomenon, illustrated in Fig. 1d [26]. This leads to a higher agglomeration of carbon dots, potentially causing a clogging effect and degradation, presenting a rough filament surface and drastic colour change, as shown in Fig. 1e. However, as the material is released from die pressure, the filament expands due to memory recovery [24,25] (Fig. 1f). To maintain a constant cooling rate, a fan is used (Fig. 1g). Once processed, the filament is rolled using a winding unit as shown in Fig. 1h. The resulting filament is a particulate composite with some micro defects shown in Fig. 3, which are generated by the non-controlled parameters of the manufacturing process and the chemical affinity between the phases. Therefore, only the first five conditions are characterised through video analysis. The filaments achieve a diameter variation ranging from 1.70 to 1.85 mm. It is noteworthy that CD amounts from 0.1 to 1 wt% result in uniform filament diameters, while CD amounts higher than 3 wt% lead to some geometric variation.

Five samples are 3D printed for each condition using a Creality® Ender 3 S1 machine. The slicing of the geometries is done using the Creality® slicer 4.8 software. The printing parameters were determined based on preliminary investigations reported by [27]. These parameters include a layer height of 0.2 mm, a shell with a wall line count of 2, a top-bottom thickness of 0.4 mm, and a top/bottom pattern with lines in one direction. The infill density is set at 100 %. The printing and build plate temperatures are set at 200 °C and 60°C, respectively. The printing speed, infill speed, and outer wall speed are set at 50 mm/s, while the travel speed is set at 150 mm/s. The printing process does not involve slow layers, and retraction is activated for travel with a 0.8 mm distance and a retraction speed of 40 mm/s. The cooling fan speed is maintained at 100 %, ensuring no material degradation. The onset degradation of the carbon dots (CDs) occurs above 200°C, while the dTG indicates a maximum degradation temperature of around 240°C, as illustrated in Fig. 2. It is important to note that the 3D printer operates at a speed of 50 mm/s, allowing the material to pass through the heated nozzle in a minimal amount of time. This brief exposure minimises the risk of degradation, in contrast to the prolonged exposure in the TGA test conditions. The tensile testing protocols adhere to the ASTM D638 [28] standard; however, the sample geometry ( Fig. 4) is adjusted to concentrate stress at the midpoint and enhance sensitivity to UV light.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of carbon dots are measured using a Hitachi U-2010 spectrophotometer, covering a range from 200 to 600 nm. Aqueous suspensions of carbon dots are analysed in a quartz cell with an optical path length of 10 mm. The molecular structure and functional groups present in carbon dots are analysed using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy with a Perkin Elmer Spectrum RXI spectrometer, employing the KBr pellet technique. High-resolution images of the carbon dots' morphology and structure are obtained using a Tecnai G2–20 Super Twin FEI microscope operating at 200 kV. The carbon dot samples are diluted and dried onto an ultrathin film with a thickness of 3 nm. TEM provides valuable insights into the size, shape, and internal structure of the carbon dots. The vibrational modes and molecular structure of carbon dots are analysed using a micro-Raman Witec Alpha300RA system. A 532 nm laser is used as the excitation wavelength. The elemental composition of carbon dots is determined using Perkin-Elmer CHN2400 equipment, where the samples are subjected to combustion in an oxygen atmosphere, and the resulting gases are analysed in a thermal conductivity detector. The fluorescence emission properties of carbon dots are recorded using a Varian Cray Eclipse spectrometer. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the carbon dots is determined by comparing their spectra to a quinine reference sample, which is dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. The comparison is performed at different concentrations, using a consistent excitation wavelength of 340 nm. To calculate the fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence intensities and absorbance values of the carbon dot sample are analysed relative to the reference substance. The following equation is employed:
(1)
∅
CD
=
∅
ST
S
CD
S
ST
η
CD
2
η
ST
2
Where, Ø is the quantum yield, CD and ST are the carbon dot and the standard sample, respectively, S is the angular coefficient and η is the refractive index.

The tensile testing is conducted using an Instron machine equipped with a 50 kN load cell. Five samples per condition are tested. Due to the small thickness and width of the sample, they are subjected to a speed of 0.25 mm/min, resulting in a failure time of approximately 4.5–5 min. The strain is obtained through the head machine displacement since the extensometer does not fit this sample size. Additionally, a UV light is positioned in front of the sample to activate the carbon dots and induce a fluorescent blue colour. This illumination technique aids in visualising and analysing the material's behaviour during testing.

For recording purposes, a digital camera is placed perpendicular to the samples. This camera captures a video of the entire testing process, enabling to observe and analyse the sample's elongation and failure. The video recording is carried out at a frame rate of 29.83 frames per second, with a resolution of 1920 frame-width and 1080 frame-height. These settings ensure a detailed and clear representation of the sample's response. During the sample elongation, a Python routine considering BGR colour space, and the maximum value of fluorescence as 100 % is developed to analyse the images extracted from the video. This routine focuses on the centre point of the sample. By examining the fluorescence level at this point along with the evolution of the width ( Fig. 5), valuable insights into the material's behaviour and characteristics can be obtained. The width dimension at the centre point of the samples is measured until failure.

The elastic modulus of extruded filaments is assessed using the ultra-microhardness test conducted on a Shimadzu® machine (DUH-211S) fitted with a Vickers indenter made of diamond material, boasting a hardness of 1140000 N/mm². During the test, an indentation speed of 35.0335 N/s is applied, and the load is held for 5 s. These parameters ensure consistent and accurate measurements of the filaments' elastic modulus calculated using the inbuilt Shimadzu® software. For comparative analysis, a 3D-printed part is also tested laterally. This provides a reference point to evaluate the mechanical properties of the extruded filaments. Fig. 6 illustrates the setup and positioning of the samples. To prepare the samples, they are embedded into a resin capsule based on the surface to be analysed. The samples are then polished using five different types of sandpaper, namely 320, 400, 600, 800, and 1200. This polishing process ensures a smooth and uniform surface for accurate measurements. To obtain reliable results, three measurements are performed for each sample. A typical loading-unloading graph is shown in Fig. 6. In addition to the ultra-microhardness testing, the embedded samples are also analysed using an optical microscope. Specifically, an Olympus BX51 microscope is used for this purpose, with a magnification of 100×. This allows for a detailed observation of the samples to identify any possible defects.

A digital microscope (DM4) is used to examine tensile specimens after testing ( Fig. 7). The DM4 is equipped with a 720-pixel screen resolution and two polarised lenses. The polarisation technique enables the observation of failure in the same spots recorded by the camera, providing valuable insights into the behaviour of the samples.

The preparation of the carbon dots has proven to be highly suitable for scaling up production to tens of grams. This method offers significant advantages over traditional high-pressure reactions conducted in stainless steel autoclaves, as highlighted in previous studies [29,30]. One notable advantage of this method is its compatibility with easy extrusion in combination with polylactic acid (PLA). Unlike conventional approaches, this method does not require high-pressure conditions but operates at a relatively low temperature of 150 °C. Additionally, it eliminates the need for solvents and avoids the requirement for purification steps. By employing this scalable approach, a single preparation of carbon dots yields an impressive 23.0 g, achieving a remarkable mass yield of 68 %.

Fig. 8a shows the FTIR spectrum. The broad band observed between 3050 and 3550 cm−1, centred at 3430 cm−1, corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds. This indicates the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups in the carbon dots. The peak observed at 1709 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of CO bonds from carboxylic acid groups [31]. The signal observed at 1655 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO stretching vibrations of amides, as well as CC ring absorptions of aromatic structures [31]. The peak observed at 1560 cm−1 is linked to the -NH bending vibrations, which are characteristic of amides and amines. The signal observed at 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of C-O bonds. This further suggests the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the carbon dots. The asymmetric stretching of C-NH-C bonds appears at 1231 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum provides valuable insights into the presence of various oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups in the carbon dots, including carboxylic acid, alcohol, amines, and amides. These functional groups are located on the surface of the nanoparticles, contributing to their high polarity. The elemental analysis results provide further evidence for the presence of oxygenated and nitrogenous functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. The atomic percentages of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and hydrogen (H) were determined to be 43.96 %, 37.26 %, 12.82 %, and 5.96 %, respectively. These percentages indicate the relative abundance of each element in the carbon dots, highlighting the significant presence of oxygen and nitrogen.

The Raman spectrum analysis, as shown in Fig. 8b, reveals two main peaks. The first peak appears at 1351 cm−1 and is known as the D band. This D band is associated with domains of disordered carbon, indicating the presence of functionalisation and defects in the carbon dots. The second peak observed at 1578 cm−1 is called the G band, which corresponds to more ordered domains of sp2 graphitised carbon [32]. The band that appears around 2830 cm−1 is related to second-order characteristics related to the combination modes D+G, 2 G, and 2D [33]. Additionally, the Raman spectrum displays an IG/ID ratio of approximately 1, which can be used to estimate the degree of graphitisation in the internal structure of the CDs. As a result, IG/ID values higher than 1 indicate a more pronounced graphitic structure, while values around 1 or lower generally indicate the presence of defects and an amorphous structural characteristic [34]. Therefore, the CDs can be classified as amorphous.

Fig. 8c provides insights into the absorption and fluorescence properties of the carbon nanoparticles at different excitation wavelengths. The sample is dispersed in water and subjected to visible light (left) and UV radiation (right). Under visible light illumination, the dispersion of carbon nanoparticles appears light yellow. This indicates that the sample exhibits absorption in the visible region, leading to the observed colour. When illuminated with UV radiation at a wavelength of 365 nm, the sample displays strong fluorescence in the blue region. This fluorescence is a characteristic property of the carbon nanoparticles and can be attributed to their unique structural and chemical composition. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the carbon nanoparticles reveals two distinct bands. The first band is observed around 350 nm, which is typical for n→π* transitions of CO bonds from different functional groups [29]. This suggests the presence of various functional groups containing CO bonds in the carbon nanoparticles. The second strong absorption band is observed around 230 nm, which can be attributed to aromatic π→π* transitions of CC bonds [29]. The fluorescence emission curves obtained at different excitation wavelengths (ranging from 240 to 380 nm) show strong emissions in the blue region of the spectrum, specifically around 455 nm. This indicates that the carbon nanoparticles exhibit fluorescence with a consistent emission wavelength regardless of the excitation wavelength used. This lack of emission peak shift suggests uniformity in both the size and energy levels of the surface states of the nanoparticles [22]. The fluorescence quantum yield of the CD sample is determined by employing the slope method in conjunction with results from UV-Vis and fluorescence measurements conducted across five distinct concentrations, using quinine as a standard. The resulting quantum yield is found to be 59.7 % ( Fig. 9a). The nanoparticles are observed to be well-dispersed and exhibit a near-spherical shape, signifying a high level of structural integrity. This observation is made through visual examination. To further characterise the nanoparticles, the average size and size distribution are measured. The measurements reveal that the carbon nanoparticles have a size range of 1.1–4.4 nm, with an average size of 2.3 nm (Fig. 9b). Fig. 8d presents a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image that effectively showcases the dispersion of nearly spherical nanoparticles distributed across the carbon microscopy grid. CDs have been highlighted with red circles due to the low contrast between the carbon nanoparticles and the ultra-thin carbon grid, evidencing their morphology and distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical testing, fluorescence and width-dimension analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the mean tensile elastic modulus (a) and strength (b) of the filaments at various concentration levels. The incorporation of carbon dots into PLA filaments results in enhanced stiffness and strength compared to the pristine condition. However, it is important to note that CD concentrations higher than 1 wt% should be avoided due to the manufacturing challenges and micro defects. The filaments composed of 0.1–0.7 % CDs shown in Fig. 10 are considered feasible for production. In the case of the 3 wt% samples, there is a notable rise in both tensile modulus (Fig. 10a) and strength (Fig. 10b). Nonetheless, the filament manufacturing process resulted in a material with an uneven diameter, causing machine overpowering and reduced stability.

Table 1 presents the mean mechanical properties, their respective standard deviation and Tukey test analysis. According to the Tukey test, similar letter groups mean statistically equivalent means. The presence of 3.0 wt% CDs results in the highest mean tensile modulus and strength. However, based on the Tukey test, concentrations of 1 and 5 wt% lead to equivalent means, as they share the letter group A. The inclusion of CD particles increases the stiffness of PLA samples, as the pristine condition falls under the letter group C. The Tukey test also reveals that samples with 0.1, 0.5, and 0.7 wt% CDs achieve statistically equivalent means. In terms of tensile strength, the pristine condition and samples with 5 wt% CDs share the lowest values, falling under the letter group D. On the other hand, samples with 3 wt% CDs achieve the highest strength (Group A), followed by concentrations of 1, 0.7, 0.3, and 0.1 wt% (letter group B). Additionally, except for samples with 1 wt% CDs, those with 0.1, 0.3, and 0.7 wt% can be grouped with samples containing 0.5 wt% CDs (Group C). In general, the incorporation of rigid particles into polymer composites leads to an increased elastic modulus but reduced strength [35]. This can be attributed to the stiffening mechanism within the elastic regime, as opposed to the increased number of interfaces and stress concentrations observed during plastic deformations. Additionally, nanoparticles can enhance the strength of polymers due to their small size and the presence of polar functional groups such as carboxylic acid and carbonyl, as referenced in [36,37]. These functional groups demonstrate strong interactions with polar polymers like PLA, thereby fostering a phenomenon of polar anchoring between the functional groups of the polymers and CDs on the surface of nanoparticles. Moreover, research has shown that polymers serve as stabilising agents for nanoparticles with high surface energy [37]. The polymeric chains tend to diffuse across the surface of nanoparticles to compensate for their inherently low surface energy. The tensile stress is dissipated to the filler particles, enabling the nanocomposite as a whole to withstand higher external stresses beyond a certain concentration at which the percolation threshold occurs. Hence, as the percolation threshold signifies the concentration of nanoparticles at which filler-filler network formation emerges or a combination of polymer-nanoparticle bridges that creates a robust 3D network, thereby enhancing the mechanical properties of the polymer, we observe that the percolation threshold in our case is attained at a concentration range from 0.1 % to 0.7 %. Beyond this concentration, mechanical properties can be improved through the formation of a robust 3D percolation network, despite the challenges in the filament manufacturing process. However, at much higher concentrations beyond the formation of the percolation network (5.0 %), due to some degree of aggregation or clustering of CDs, the mechanical properties of the composite deteriorate.

Fig. 11 shows the typical tensile stress-strain curves for each configuration, highlighting the substantial alteration in mechanical behaviour caused by the inclusion of CDs in PLA filaments. In all configurations, a linear regime is followed by a non-linear region and yielding, except for pristine PLA filaments, which exhibit a remarkably smooth yielding process. This supports the mechanism of mechanical enhancement achieved through the incorporation of rigid particles into polymers, which results in reduced ductility.

The samples depicted in Fig. 11 are used for the fluorescence and sample width analysis, as shown in
Figs. 12–13. Fig. 12 presents the fluorescence and width analysis for 0.1 and 0.3 wt% CD-reinforced PLA samples. The samples composed of 0.1 wt% CD reveal a linear elastic behaviour up to 2000 frames (Fig. 12a), which corresponds to the fluorescence trend rise (Fig. 12b). Approximately at 7000 frames, there is a visible change in the mechanical behaviour, characterised by a small overshoot in fluorescence. As the plastic deformation increases (1.15–1.65 %), both the fluorescence signal and width level decrease. After reaching the maximum stress and undergoing long yielding (7500–8000 frames), the fluorescence signal (Fig. 12b) and width level (Fig. 12c) decrease drastically. In general, samples made with 0.3 wt% CDs (Fig. 12d-f) behave similarly to those composed of 0.1 wt%, however with increased fluorescence signal at the plastic regime. The decrease in fluorescence signal and width dimension with increasing deformation can be attributed to the increase in the relative distances between CDs, weakening their fluorescence effect, especially when a lower concentration of CDs is considered. It is worth noting that the width dimension plateau is more pronounced in the case of 0.1 wt% CD samples, indicating the effect of transverse stiffening by the CD inclusions. As shown in Table 1, the 0.1 wt% CD samples lead to a slightly higher elastic modulus compared to the 0.3 wt% CD samples, which can be attributed to a better homogenisation of the nanoparticles within the PLA matrix.

The fluorescence levels for samples containing 0.7 and 1.0 wt% CDs (Fig. 13) exhibit noticeable differences compared to samples with 0.1 and 0.3 wt% concentrations, particularly during the plastic region. This phenomenon requires further investigation for a better understanding. The authors hypothesise that at higher CD concentrations, there may be overlapped clusters of particles distributed across the thickness of the sample. Subsequently, during plastic elongation, the particles separate, resulting in increased luminescence, as depicted in Fig. 13b,e. Furthermore, Fig. 13c,f illustrate that the width dimension decreases at approximately 6000 frames for 0.7 wt% CDs and 7000 frames for 1.0 wt% CDs, indicating transverse stiffening of the samples when a larger amount of CDs is incorporated. Furthermore, Table 1 highlights that the 1.0 wt% CD samples exhibit higher elastic modulus, strength, and toughness compared to the 0.7 wt% CD samples.

In Fig. 14, the impact of sample elongation on the fluorescence level is depicted. The horizontal direction of the images corresponds to the loading direction. It is observed a transverse crack propagation in a 0.5 wt% CD sample. In samples containing 1.0 wt% and 3.0 wt% of CDs, a necking effect is evident, affecting the fluorescence level and resulting in a smoother appearance. In contrast, samples containing 5 wt% CDs present a brittle failure mode, as shown in Fig. 14d. This can be attributed to the higher concentration of CDs in the matrix, which enhances transverse stiffness but weakens interfacial bonding between the 3D-printed layers. Effective load transfer and mechanical integrity rely on strong interfacial bonding. Suboptimal interfacial bonding creates stress concentration points at the interfaces between printed threads. During tensile loading, these points serve as sites for crack initiation and propagation. In transverse rupture, weak interfacial bonds are unable to withstand transverse stresses, leading to crack propagation perpendicular to the loading direction. Fluorescence analysis indicates that the quantity and packing of CDs influence stress distribution within the filament. Poor interfacial bonding, particularly at higher CD concentrations, exacerbates stress concentration at thread interfaces, resulting in transverse rupture. This underscores the importance of optimising interfacial bonding to enhance mechanical performance and prevent premature failure of the composite material.

In order to enhance the analysis of the failure mode depicted in Fig. 14, a polarised lens is employed along with the digital microscope to provide additional information. Samples composed of 0.1 wt% CDs do not exhibit any specific pattern ( Fig. 15a). However, certain patterns become apparent in samples composed of 0.3 wt% CDs. For instance, the incorporation of 0.3 wt% CDs results in diagonal necking and debonding (Fig. 15b), indicating poor adhesion. Samples with 0.5 wt% CDs demonstrate a fragile transverse fracture with longitudinal crack propagation, suggesting that the failure may have initiated from the centre and propagated toward the edges (Fig. 15c). In samples comprising 0.7 wt% CDs, thread unsticks, and the shearing effect are more pronounced along the sample (Fig. 15d). A similar shearing pattern is observed in samples with 1.0 wt% CDs (Fig. 15e), although it is not well-defined as in the case of 0.7 wt%. The sample fabricated with 3 wt% CDs reveals a darker region at the edges, indicating a higher stress concentration. Finally, samples containing 5.0 wt% CD samples exhibit only brittle failure which may be attributed to the substantial amount of rigid CDs. It is noteworthy that providing a detailed explanation for each case is challenging due to the involvement of the parameters of two manufacturing processes. The following paragraphs delve into proposing and exploring failure mechanisms to better understand the mechanical behaviour of CD-reinforced PLA polymers.

The findings indicate that the mechanical behaviour of CD-reinforced PLA polymers is not solely determined by CD concentrations. The interfacial adhesion between parallel threads, resulting from the extrusion and 3D printing processes, also plays a significant role in the macromechanical performance. The packing distribution of CDs within the filaments is a critical factor that affects mechanical properties and failure modes. It is observed that the incorporation of CDs into PLA polymers increases their stiffness and modulus of toughness, as shown in Table 1. However, considering the similarity of the mean mechanical data, the choice of the optimal CD concentration can be based on the observed failure mode. The quantity of CDs influences rheology, particle packing, and their movement under mechanical loads.
Figs. 16 and 17 demonstrate different deformation mechanisms for low and high CD concentrations, respectively. For the same base material (PLA + CDs), two distinct failure modes, i.e. brittle and ductile, are achieved, indicating a significant difference in microstructures. Reference [35] suggests that the strengthening mechanism resulting from the presence of particles under tensile loads can be attributed to the increase in transverse rigidity, facilitated by the presence of well-packed stiff particles, which delays longitudinal deformation and neck formation.

Samples containing 0.5 wt% CDs exhibit a brittle transverse fracture with longitudinal crack propagation. This failure mode suggests that this particle concentration can support high tensile loads with minimal transverse deformations. However, it also transfers a portion of the stress concentration to the interfacial zone between threads without causing failure. This mechanism indicates that CDs achieve optimal particle packing throughout the sample, as depicted in Fig. 16a. As the CDs come closer to each other under indirect compressive transverse loads, they become more tightly packed (Fig. 16b) until achieving full contact. Consequently, the matrix phase becomes overloaded, particularly at the centre point (as shown in Fig. 16c), leading to crack propagation (Fig. 16e) along the transverse direction with a subsequent brittle rupture, as shown in Figs. 14a and 15c.

The interfacial zone between threads is illustrated in Fig. 17. As mentioned earlier, the quantity and packing of CDs within the filament have an impact on the stress concentration and deformation mode at the thread interfacial zone. Initially, the CDs come closer to each other within the filament, subsequently affecting the interfacial zone, which consists of a significant amount of PLA, as shown in Fig. 17b. At this stage, the failure mode of the 3D-printed samples is determined based on their physical characteristics and uniformity. It is hypothesized that if the CDs are well packed and distributed within the filament, crack propagation tends to occur in the transverse direction, as seen in Fig. 16. When physical inconsistencies occur between parallel threads, they begin to rotate, resulting in shearing stress, increased longitudinal deformation, and neck formation, as shown in Fig. 17c and Fig. 17d. Moreover, higher CD concentrations lead to cluster formation, compromising the load transfer functionality of the matrix phase, and consequently causing a shift to a ductile fracture mode.

Fig. 18 depicts the outcomes of dynamic microhardness (DUH) testing for both the 3D-printed component (3D) and filaments composed of 0.1–1.0 wt% CDs. The testing encompasses both the cross-sectional (vertical) and lateral (horizontal) aspects of the filaments. Fig. 18a displays Young's modulus, while Fig. 18b represents the indentation depth. It is observed that Young's modulus and indentation depth exhibit opposite behaviours. The elastic modulus data obtained from tensile testing (Table 1) align with the microhardness testing, yielding a value of approximately 3.5 GPa. 3D samples containing 1 wt% CDs experience a significantly similar stiffness compared to 0.5, which can be attributed to an excess of particles in the system, as well as the second melting effect. The presence of an excessive number of particles can disrupt the matrix structure and hinder the load transfer between the CDs and the polymer matrix, leading to a reduction in stiffness. Additionally, the second melting effect, resulting from the heat generated during the 3D printing process, can further contribute to the decrease in stiffness by affecting the overall material properties. The DUH data reveals considerable variability attributed to micro-scale testing, strongly influenced by particle distribution. In the case of the 3D printed samples, there is a discernible pattern of escalating Young's modulus beyond 0.3 wt%, suggesting enhanced homogenisation and diminished anisotropy. Conversely, vertically tested samples exhibit a higher Young's modulus compared to horizontally tested ones, indicating clear anisotropy. Furthermore, Vickers hardness demonstrates an increase with the 3D printed samples, while the other two sample types do not exhibit a clear distinction. Furthermore, the indentations are performed on smooth surfaces without micro defects, which may have affected the sample behaviour during tensile testing. Fig. 19 presents the microstructural analysis of samples containing 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 wt% CDs. It is observed that as the amount of CDs increases, some defects, such as voids, become more prominent on the surfaces of the samples.

The carbon dots were obtained using a simple method suitable for scaling up the production of carbon nanoparticles. Their incorporation into PLA material enhances its mechanical performance, particularly in terms of stiffness, compared to its original state. Remarkably, adding 0.5 wt% of carbon dots significantly enhances the final 3D printed products. These products achieved a brittle transverse fracture with longitudinal crack propagation, indicating improved particle packing and strong tensile load support with minimal transverse deformations. Conversely, concentrations of 1, 3, and 5 wt% should be avoided due to manufacturing challenges and the potential for micro-defects. Low luminescence is observed due to the presence of clusters. However, increasing deformation results in enhanced fluorescence due to the separation of particles. At concentrations of 0.1 and 0.3 wt%, there is a reduction in fluorescence signal and width dimension, attributed to an increase in the relative distance between the carbon dots, weakening their fluorescence effect. At the micro level, microhardness testing demonstrated a correlation with tensile testing, except for 3D composites at higher carbon dot levels. This deviation can be attributed to the additional homogenisation achieved through the 3D printing process. The mechanical performance of the composite filaments is significantly influenced by the interfacial adhesion between parallel 3D printed threads, resulting in different failure modes, including brittle to ductile fractures, shearing patterns, and detachments of the 3D printed threads at the interfacial zones. The enhanced PLA filaments are suitable for creating both solid and lattice 3D-printed products. Specifically, lattices with millimetre-scale unit cells are well-suited for lightweight applications, including drone structures. In this scenario, the recommendation is to use PLA reinforced with 0.5 wt% carbon dots, which achieves approximately 30 MPa in tensile strength, 3.5 GPa in elastic modulus, and facilitates easy manufacturing. Further investigations will be carried out to evaluate rheological additives for enhancing the flow within the extruder barrel to enable the production of filaments with high concentrations of carbon dots. Additionally, the surface of the CDs can be customized to modify the surface groups, which can be accomplished by adjusting the synthesis conditions or through post-synthesis functionalisation, ultimately improving their dispersion and interaction within the polymer matrix.

Alberto Jorge Baeza Campuzano: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rafael Barbosa Rezende: Investigation, Formal analysis. Nestor Cifuentes Taborda: Investigation. Júlio César dos Santos: Methodology, Investigation. Fabiano Vargas Pereira: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tulio Panzera: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

Alberto Jorge Baeza Campuzano reports financial support was provided by Minas Gerais State Foundation of Support to the Research. Fabiano Vargas Pereira reports financial support was provided by Minas Gerais State Foundation of Support to the Research. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
